verses.text: ‘Then answered Zophar the Naamathite, and said.’ The third friend takes his second turn. Zophar is the harshest of the three, the one most committed to the idea that Job must be hiding sin. Each speech-tag repeats the same form, but the speakers are not the same. The book of Job stages three distinct theological errors, not one.

‘My thoughts cause me to answer.’ The Hebrew sa‘ipim — disquieting thoughts — evokes inner agitation. Zophar admits his speech rises from his own restlessness more than from careful theology. Speakers often reveal more about themselves than about the topic they are addressing. The whole book of Job suggests that this is especially true when we speak about other people’s pain.

‘I have heard the check of my reproach.’ Zophar has been stung by Job’s previous words. The Hebrew muthar musari — the chastisement of my shame — admits his ego is bruised. Notice how often theological argument disguises hurt feelings. Even in defending God, Zophar is in some part defending himself.

‘Knowest thou not this of old?’ Zophar appeals to ancient wisdom. The Hebrew minni-qedem — from of old — invokes immemorial tradition. He is sure the wicked have always come to bad ends quickly. But Job’s own life is the counterexample, sitting in front of him. Tradition can be true in general and still wrong about a particular case.

‘The triumphing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment.’ Zophar’s claim sounds proverbial; sometimes it is true. Yet the book of Job exists partly to challenge such tidy formulas. Wickedness sometimes prospers for a lifetime (Ps 73). Theology must hold both Zophar’s general truth and Asaph’s harder observation.

Zophar imagines a wicked man reaching the heavens — and then plummeting (verse 7 will continue the image). The picture is borrowed from Ezekiel 28’s prince of Tyre or Isaiah 14’s morning star. Pride at altitude is fragile. The theology is sound; the misapplication — calling Job that wicked man — is the tragedy.
20:7 thrown away like their own dung: Cp. 1 Kgs 14:9-11; 2 Kgs 9:36-37; Ps 83:10; Jer 8:1-2.

20:10 Because stolen riches are ill-gotten, they provide no lasting benefit (20:18-21).

20:14-16 poisonous venom . . . cobras . . . viper: In the end, the wicked are no longer deadly to others (Matt 3:7; Rom 3:13) but only to themselves (Prov 23:29-35).

20:17 The streams of olive oil and rivers of milk and honey signify superabundant blessing (Exod 5:19; Deut 27:3; Joel 3:18).

20:20 Like the grave or death (Prov 27:20; 30:15-16), the wicked are always greedy and never satisfied (Eccl 5:19; Isa 57:20-21; Hab 2:5).

20:24 The wicked cannot escape trouble (cp. Isa 24:17-18; Jer 15:2-3; Amos 5:19; 9:1-2). When they try to escape one threat, they fall victim to another.

20:25 terrors of death: Although the words “of death” are not in the Hebrew, they are implied because the arrowhead glistens with blood (literally with gall), indicating a deadly hit on a vital organ (6:4; 16:13; Ps 7:12).
20:26 Images such as deepest darkness and wildfire are often associated with God’s presence (Deut 4:11; 5:22-23; Heb 12:18) and especially with his judgment (Exod 9:23-24; 10:21-29).
20:27 heavens . . . will testify: Cp. Deut 4:26; 30:19; 31:28.
